In today’s ‘First Draft,’ we ponder what “important update” the president will have for us about his war, which is going even worse than you think; Americans don’t approve of Trump’s fascist paramilitary; and AOC clarifies her stance on aid for Israel.

Fool to Address the Nation

When Donald Trump launched his dangerous and very illegal war against Iran, he broke with modern presidential tradition. Rather than give a live primetime address, as is customary, he posted a prerecorded video from Mar-a-Lago. Tonight, he will finally give his first live primetime address about the war… on April Fool’s Day…. begging the question: Is he messing with us?

White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt tweeted Tuesday that “President Trump will give an Address to the Nation to provide an important update on Iran.”

What should you, dear Zeteo reader, expect to hear from America’s first Mad King on Wednesday at 9pm ET? Let’s break down the various options:

Option 1: Trump will, George W. Bush-style, declare “Mission Accomplished” and cut his losses while pretending he won. This means forgetting about trying to gain control over the Strait of Hormuz – which Iran has shut down, blocking the flow of oil and throttling the global economy. Under this scenario, the war can only be seen as a major economic victory for the Iranian regime; it becomes the “tollbooth operator” for the world’s most important waterway, and looks like more of a regional power.

Option 2: Trump will declare a ceasefire and announce he’s negotiating an end to the war. According to the Associated Press, “US officials are pushing for direct Iran talks after failed indirect efforts,” and there is discussion about having these talks in Pakistan because there are “no US airbases, so [it’s] safer.”

Option 3: Trump will use his primetime address to finally attempt to sell the American public on his historically unpopular, reckless, unnecessary, and unauthorized war – and try to present a coherent rationale for why he launched it. (Good luck with that!)

Option 4: Trump announces the US is invading Kharg Island, and troops are landing as he speaks. Our belligerent president has wanted to seize the island, the site of most of Iran’s oil exports, for decades. It’s an incredibly dangerous idea. The administration knows this mission would be a bloodbath and would result in “considerable” US casualties. It’s not clear what it would accomplish.

Option 5: The president spends an hour telling Americans all about… his White House ballroom project. Over the weekend, Trump was speaking with reporters on Air Force One about how he delivered “regime change” in Iran when he started showing them new renderings of the ballroom. “We just got these in from the architects,” he said, marveling that it will be “the same height as the White House.” That was before a judge halted construction on Tuesday, siding with “a Radical Left Group of Lunatics,” as Trump put it.

Today could be a historic day in Trumpland, no matter what the president says at night.

In the morning, Trump is expected to head to the Supreme Court to hear oral arguments on his flatly unconstitutional executive order attempting to eliminate birthright citizenship as guaranteed under the 14th Amendment. “I’m going,” Trump said Tuesday. No president in the history of the United States has physically turned up at the Supreme Court to hear arguments being made.

Nor has any president handled a foreign war so badly – and that includes George W. Bush. In the days leading up to Wednesday’s primetime address, Trump administration officials and others close to the White House told my Zeteo colleague, Asawin “Swin” Suebsaeng, that no matter what the president and his goons say, the war is rotting them from within. “However poorly you think the war is going, it is worse,” one senior administration official said in recent days, describing an atmosphere of dread, poor morale, and surging anxiety over what would happen if Trump decides to invade.

Various US officials have assessed that the Trump administration has engineered a “lose-lose” situation, as one of them puts it: Trump either extends or escalates his illegal war – and continues bleeding public support and losing American soldiers – or he tries to pull out and save face, and everyone knows he got his ass kicked by a less powerful enemy.

Another US official predicted a coming “humiliation” for Team Trump, even if Trump tries to pull the plug on his war effort and declare “victory.”

A different senior official says, “Iran can declare victory, too,” adding that practically “nobody will buy our attempt to sell this as a big win.”

Numerous Trump advisers and government officials bemoaned to Swin that a very likely outcome of Trump’s war on Iran is going to be handing the Iranian regime a propaganda win of credibly telling the world that it beat President Trump and the Americans.

We’ll see on Wednesday which option Trump chooses: pretend he won, look for an off-ramp, escalate the war, or pivot to talking about his ballroom. It’s April Fool’s Day, after all.

🇮🇷 Iran War Updates

Death tolls:

Iran: Iran’s deputy health minister told Al Jazeera that at least 1,937 people had been killed in US-Israeli attacks. Iranian officials said earlier this month that nearly 20,000 people had been injured.

Lebanon: Israel has killed at least 1,268 people, including 125 children, in Lebanon, according to yesterday’s figures from the country’s Health Ministry.

US troops: At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.

Israel: At least 29 people, including 10 Israeli soldiers, have been killed and thousands injured in Iranian and Hezbollah attacks.

Elsewhere in the region: Dozens of others have been killed across the region, including four Palestinian women in the occupied West Bank.

And in more Iran war news…

End game?: Trump said the war was “coming to an end,” adding: “I think in two weeks or maybe a few days longer, we’ll do the job.” He also mocked Iranian threats to US tech companies.

No to NATO: Trump told the UK’s Telegraph that he was seriously considering pulling the US out of NATO. “I was never swayed by NATO. I always knew they were a paper tiger, and Putin knows that too, by the way,” he said. Earlier, he had lashed out at allies, including the UK, for not joining his war, posting, “the USA won’t be there to help you anymore.”

Positive Pete: As Trump first considered striking Iran, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth “downplayed the inherent risks,” CNN reported. It also detailed the former Fox host’s relentless focus on being the TV “face” of Trump’s war.

Strike denial: The US military denied hitting Lamerd in Iran on Feb. 28, killing at least 21, including girls in a sports hall. The New York Times said it was a US strike, showing “an apparent tungsten pellet” from a Precision Strike Missile.

Lebanon latest: Israel will destroy “all homes in villages near the border … according to the model of Rafah and Beit Hanoun in Gaza,” Defense Minister Israel Katz said, referring to two cities completely decimated by Israel.

Peacekeeper deaths: Two Indonesian troops in Lebanon were killed and one severely injured by a roadside bomb, the UN’s peacekeeping chief said. Three Indonesians have now died since Israel attacked Hezbollah.

ICE Disliked

Americans are evenly split on whether they approve of how Trump has handled immigration at the southern US border, according to a new UMass Amherst national poll. (Read more UMass poll results here.) They oppose how he’s handled the deportation of undocumented immigrants, 47% to 54%.

What Americans really dislike about Trump’s approach to immigration is the violent, masked secret police he’s deployed throughout the country to terrorize noncitizens and citizens alike.

The UMass poll finds 33% of Americans approve of how ICE is doing its job, and 59% disapprove. There is very limited support for common ICE practices: only 29% of Americans support ICE agents wearing masks; 28% support ICE agents using facial recognition to identify US citizens protesting ICE activities; 26% support ICE agents using military-grade weapons; 16% support ICE agents using physical force against protesters; and 13% support ICE agents arresting US citizens for observing the actions of ICE agents.

Asked whether ICE officers are adequately trained to conduct immigration enforcement efforts in American cities, just 29% agree.

The poll, by YouGov for UMass, included a sample of 1,000 Americans and was conducted from March 20-25.

🗞️ What You Need to Know

AOC fronts up: At a Democratic Socialists of America forum in New York, Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez committed to voting against “any spending on arms for Israel, including so-called defensive capabilities.”

SAVE Act lite: Trump signed an executive order attempting to tighten mail-in voting by creating a nationwide list of eligible voters and mandating the US Postal Service monitor citizens who receive such ballots in federal elections.

Public media win: A federal judge blocked Trump’s order to cease funding NPR and PBS. Judge Randolph Moss wrote: “It is difficult to conceive of clearer evidence that a government action is targeted at viewpoints that the President does not like and seeks to squelch.”

Purge pushback: Three more ex-FBI agents are suing FBI Director Kash Patel for allegedly firing them for investigating Trump’s attempt to overturn the 2020 election. Patel faces multiple suits over firings that many see as political payback.

Supreme Court rules: Only Justice Ketanji Brown Jackson voted to uphold a Colorado law banning conversion therapy for LGBTQ+ youth, the other justices siding with a Christian counselor who argues the ban violates her First Amendment rights.

Checks out: Kristi Noem’s husband, Bryon, was revealed to have posed with fake breasts in pictures sent to fetish models. A representative for Noem, who tried to ban college drag shows as governor, said she was “devastated” and asked for “privacy and prayers.”

🧠 Pop Quiz!

In 1994, beloved PBS show Sesame Street featured “real estate tycoon” Ronald Grump threatening to demolish the street for a tower and berating puppets, including Bert, Ernie, and Telly. Which iconic New York actor played him?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

🌏 Anywhere But America

🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza: Philippe Lazzarini marked the end of his tenure as the head of the UN Palestinian refugee agency (UNRWA) by calling for a UN investigation into the killing of more than 390 UNRWA employees in Gaza. "I believe that we need to have a panel – a high-level panel of experts to look into the killing of our staff,” he said.

🇮🇶 Iraq kidnapping: American reporter Shelly Kittleson was kidnapped in Baghdad. The US blamed a pro-Iranian militia. Kittleson’s employer, Al-Monitor, said it was “deeply alarmed” and called for her “safe and immediate release.”

🇺🇦 Solemn ceremony: European foreign ministers visited Bucha, near Kyiv, to mark the fourth anniversary of atrocities by invading Russian forces. Meanwhile, Ukraine continued to use drones to attack Russian oil facilities in the Baltic region.

🇨🇺 Tanker docks: A Russian tanker holding 730,000 barrels of oil reached Cuba as the US temporarily lifted its embargo. Cuba’s foreign minister denounced “the criminal cruelty of imperialism against a nation that refuses to be dominated.”

🇸🇩 ‘No safe place for women’: Documenting sexual violence in Darfur in Sudan’s civil war, Doctors Without Borders found “no safe places for women and girls.” From January 2024 to November 2025, at least 3,396 victims of sexual violence sought MSF help.

😭 WTF?!

At a Pentagon briefing, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth described with perhaps disturbing relish a visit to US Central Command. A “junior airman,” he said, “simply looked up at me with a sly smile on her face” and said she needed “More bombs, sir; and bigger bombs.” Hegseth said he would “happily oblige.”

🧠 Trivia answer: Joe Pesci.

ICYMI From Zeteo

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly and Akshay Gokul contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘manage subscription’ in Substack, scrolling down, and toggling the button for ‘First Draft’ off.

item.title: Kharg Island: Everything You Need to Know About the Island Trump Wants to Invade
item.description: As the US-Israeli war with Iran enters its fifth week, Donald Trump is openly talking about seizing a tiny island most Americans had never heard of, and it turns out he first floated the idea 38 years ago. Kharg Island – a five-mile-long coral outcrop in the Persian Gulf that houses Iran’s vital…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
As the US-Israeli war with Iran enters its fifth week, Donald Trump is openly talking about seizing a tiny island most Americans had never heard of, and it turns out he first floated the idea 38 years ago.

Kharg Island – a five-mile-long coral outcrop in the Persian Gulf that houses Iran’s vital oil export terminal – could soon become ground zero for what would be the most reckless escalation of the conflict yet.

As Zeteo has reported, senior Trump administration officials have been briefed on casualty estimates for a Kharg ground invasion, and in multiple scenarios, American losses would be “considerable.”

So, here are 7 things you should know about Kharg Island…

1. Welcome to the ‘Forbidden Island’

Kharg Island sits about 20 miles off Iran’s northern coast in the Persian Gulf. It’s only about five miles long and three miles wide, barely a speck on a map compared to Iran’s over 630,000 square miles of land.

Kharg is officially restricted; there are no tourists, no journalists, and no visits to the island without government clearance. It’s earned the nickname “Forbidden Island” because the only people allowed on it are oil workers, military personnel, and around 8,000 residents.

Satellite images of Kharg show rows of massive storage tanks, flames shooting from flare stacks, and a web of pipelines running to vast piers where supertankers dock. Beneath all that infrastructure? 2,400-year-old rock carvings, ruins of one of the oldest Christian monasteries in the Persian Gulf, and an 18th-century Dutch East India Company fort. Iranian writer Jalal Al-e-Ahmad once called it “the orphan pearl of the Persian Gulf.”

2. It’s Iran’s Economic Lifeline

Roughly 90 percent of Iran’s crude oil exports pass through Kharg’s terminals, about 1.5 million barrels per day, with much of it shipped to China. Most of Iran’s coastline is too shallow for the supertankers that haul crude around the world, so the country pumps nearly all of its production through underwater pipelines to Kharg’s deep-water port, where up to 10 supertankers can all load up at once.

As Dania Thafer, executive director of the Gulf International Forum, put it: “Kharg Island is a lifeline for Iran’s economy.” If the US were to take it, the thinking goes, it could choke the Islamic Republic’s primary revenue source.

3. Trump Floated Taking Kharg in 1988

In a 1988 interview withThe Guardian, Donald Trump, then a 41-year-old real estate mogul, sat down with journalist Polly Toynbee, and declared, “If I want to be president, then I’ll be president.” Then he laid out his foreign policy vision: “I’d be harsh on Iran… One bullet shot at one of our men or ships, and I’d do a number on Kharg Island. I’d go in and take it.”

That was 38 years ago. Trump was a casino operator with zero foreign policy experience, floating a potential presidential run at the same time as the 1980’s Iran-Iraq “Tanker War.” Fast forward to today, Trump recently called Kharg “the little oil island” that sits there, “so totally unprotected,” and told theFinancial Times that “maybe we take Kharg Island, maybe we don’t.”

After nearly four decades, his plan hasn’t evolved at all.

4. The US Already Bombed It

On March 13, the US launched what Trump called “one of the most powerful bombing raids in the history of the Middle East” against Kharg. Throughout the island, more than 90 military targets were struck, including air defenses, a naval base, mine storage facilities, the airport, and a hovercraft base. Trump said the strikes spared the Island’s oil infrastructure “for reasons of decency,” then threatened to strike it “a few more times just for fun.”

But satellite imagery taken days later, by the European Space Agency’s Sentinel-1, showed tankers still loading crude at Kharg’s terminals. And according to TankerTrackers.com, the island had been loading tankers “non-stop since the war broke out.” Does this sound familiar? If it does, that’s because…

5. Saddam Tried Destroying Kharg, and Failed

During the Iran-Iraq War in the 1980s, Saddam Hussein’s air force repeatedly bombed Kharg for years, trying to cut off Iran’s oil revenue, as part of what’s known as “the Tanker War.” Iraqi jets armed with French-made Exocet missiles hit an oil terminal originally built by the American company Amoco, destroying storage tanks and damaging infrastructure.

Iran kept repairing them and kept exporting. When facilities were knocked offline, Tehran shifted exports to backup terminals at Lavan and Sirri Islands. Since the 1980s, Iran has spent decades fortifying Kharg, which today exports about 1.5 million barrels per day, with more hardened infrastructure, underground storage, and layered defenses designed to keep oil flowing under sustained attacks.

6. An Invasion Could Be Catastrophic

Speaking about Kharg Island, Trump said this week, “I don’t think they have any defense. We could take it very easily.”

Many experts say otherwise. Kharg is just about 20 miles from Iran’s mountainous coast, and is well within range of Iranian missiles, drones, rockets, and artillery. Michael Eisenstadt of the Washington Institute told the Associated Press that drones could slip through mountain passes before radar picks them up, adding: “And we don’t have the warning time.” Bryan Clark at the Hudson Institute also warned that Iranian forces have likely booby-trapped the island with IEDs, and reasoned that once the US comes, oil production is lost anyway, so they might as well inflict casualties.

Danny Citrinowicz, who is an Iran expert at Israel’s Institute for National Security Studies, saidthat Kharg “will be hard to take. It will be hard to hold. And it might damage the economy, but not in a way that will force the Iranians to capitulate.”

Retired Rear Admiral Mark Montgomery told Axios: “If we seize Kharg Island, they’re going to turn off the spigot on the other end. It’s not like we control their oil production.”

Former Defense Intelligence Agency analyst Harrison Mann, who writes for Zeteo, has described the potential Kharg operation as “somewhere between a suicide mission and a self-imposed hostage crisis.”

Mann has argued that for Tehran, “the opportunity to inflict a mass casualty event that could sap limited US public support for the war, or to hold entire battalions as de facto hostages, may well appear more valuable to Tehran than oil revenue.”

Joe Kent, Trump’s former counterterrorism director who resigned over the war, told theWashington Post that putting troops on Kharg “would essentially be giving Iran a bunch of hostages on an island that they could barrage with drones and missiles.”

7. So What Might Actually Happen?

Right now, thousands of US troops are heading to the region; 2,500 Marines recently arrived; 1,000 paratroopers from the 82nd Airborne are expected soon; another 2,500 Marines are being deployed from California; and the USS Tripoli, an amphibious assault ship, is heading toward the Persian Gulf.

The Trump administration won’t say what these forces will do. But several analysts say a naval blockade, stopping tankers from leaving Kharg, would achieve the same goal without putting troops on a tiny island within missile range of a hostile coastline. And if the expressed “goal” of this conflict is really supposed to be helping the Iranian people, Clayton Seigle at the Center for Strategic and International Studies argued, crippling “Iran’s revenue-generating potential for many years to come would definitely not work in that direction.”

There are some signs that Trump may be looking for a way out. He has twice postponed his threatened strikes on Iran’s power grid, first on March 23, before extending that deadline to April 6.

Even the extremely hawkish Senator Lindsey Graham – who favorably compared a Kharg invasion to Iwo Jima just last week – urged Trump on Monday to “wind down the war.” However, Trump told theFinancial Times that his “favorite thing is to take the oil in Iran,” and subsequently that same day threatened to “completely obliterate” Iran’s energy infrastructure. He could change his mind at any time.

The world’s least-prepared president is workshopping the same bad idea he had during the Reagan administration, only now he has a military to actually do something with it.

Zeteo’s Andrew McDonald contributed to this piece.

Check out more from Zeteo:

item.title: 7 Ways Israel Is Turning Lebanon Into Gaza
item.description: Israel isn’t hiding it. In the last month, its far-right government has been explicitly clear that it plans to do to southern Lebanon and parts of Beirut what it did to Gaza. Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich has warned that Beirut’s southern suburbs of Dahiyeh will soon look “like Khan Younis,” a…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Israel isn’t hiding it. In the last month, its far-right government has been explicitly clear that it plans to do to southern Lebanon and parts of Beirut what it did to Gaza.

Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich has warned that Beirut’s southern suburbs of Dahiyeh will soon look “like Khan Younis,” a city in Gaza that Israel has left almost completely uninhabitable.

Defense Minister Israel Katz has said he and Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu instructed the Israeli military to destroy the infrastructure near Lebanon’s border as it did in Rafah and Beit Hanoun, two cities in Gaza that Israel completely decimated.

We’re already seeing exactly how Israel’s plan is playing out. With the death toll already at more than 1,260, here are seven ways Israel is using its genocidal Gaza playbook to kill, decimate communities, and take over parts of Lebanon while flaunting international law in the process.

1. Killing Journalists

Over the weekend, Israel killed three Lebanese journalists – Fatima Ftouni, her brother Mohammed Ftouni, and Ali Shuaib – in targeted strikes on their vehicle, which was reportedly clearly marked as press. As it’s done in Gaza, where it’s killed over 250 Palestinian journalists, Israel attempted to justify its attack by claiming one of the reporters, Shuaib, was a militant.

But also, as it’s done after killing journalists in Gaza, Israel offered no tangible proof. Hezbollah rejected Israel’s claim that the journalist was part of its elite Rawdan Force. In an extremely rare admission, Israel told Fox it had photoshopped a photo showing Shuaib in a military uniform. “Unfortunately there isn’t really a picture of it, it was photoshopped,” the Israeli military said.

Israel, of course, said nothing of Fatima and Mohammed Ftouni.

2. Targeting Health Workers and Infrastructure

Since Oct. 7, Israel has killed more than 1,700 health workers across Gaza in what UN experts have labeled a “medicide.” We’re now witnessing another medicide take place in Lebanon.

Lebanese officials say that in just the last four weeks, Israel has killed more than 50 healthcare workers, targeted scores of ambulances and emergency service workers, and forced five hospitals to close, including, as the UN points out, four with maternity wards. One hospital in the southern city of Tyre has been hit at least five times, according to Al Jazeera. Nine hospitals have been damaged, and more than 50 primary healthcare centers have been forced to close, per the UN.

Israel uses the same types of allegations it uses in Gaza to attempt to justify its actions. It claims Hezbollah is using ambulances and medical facilities for military purposes. Of course, as we know from Gaza, these claims are baseless (remember the “Hamas headquarters” Israel insisted was under Al-Shifa hospital in Gaza?)

Regardless, targeting healthcare facilities unless there’s definitive proof that they’re being used for military purposes renders such infrastructure protected under international law, experts say.

3. Genocidal Language

Just as we saw in the opening days of Israel’s war on Gaza, Israeli politicians and commentators are making zero effort to mask their genocidal rhetoric when it comes to Israel’s assault on Lebanon.

“The current war in Lebanon must end with a radical change, beyond the vanquishing of the terror group Hezbollah,” Smotrich has said, per Israeli media. “The Litani must be our new border with the state of Lebanon.”

Leaflets dropped by the Israeli military in Beirut said, “In light of the resounding success in Gaza, the New Reality Newspaper arrives in Lebanon,” in what experts have labeled a form of psychological warfare.

Former Israeli Defense Minister Yoav Gallant, who infamously called Palestinians “animals” at the start of the Gaza war, urged Israel to “strike and eliminate everything that’s in Dahiyah, Baalbek, Tyre, Sidon, Nabatieh, everywhere,” referring to cities in Lebanon.

4. Forced ‘Evacuations’ and ‘Buffer Zones’

Since the start of its escalation in Lebanon in early March, Israel has issued so-called evacuation orders to more than 1.2 million people in Lebanon, including the entire area south of the Litani River. Like in Gaza, these orders effectively amount to ‘leave or risk being killed’ orders.

To be clear, Israel has a long history of invading southern Lebanon, forcing residents from their villages, and occupying large swaths of land. But as we know from the Gaza playbook, residents will not be allowed to return to their homes (or what exists of them), likely indefinitely.

In fact, Israeli Defense Minister Israel Katz made quite clear this week that Israel plans to occupy the area south of the Litani River, which is about 19 miles north of the Israeli-Lebanese border, even after the latest escalation ends.

Israel likes to call these areas “buffer zones” necessary for Israel’s security, but what the government is effectively doing is redrawing its country’s borders in its quest for ‘Greater Israel.’

5. Cutting Off Access to Water and Humanitarian Aid

Israel’s invasion and targeting of bridges over the Litani River connecting the south to the north is isolating nearly a tenth of Lebanese territory from the rest of the country – keeping anyone who fled from reaching their homes, but also cutting off access to necessary humanitarian aid for anyone who stayed, aid groups warn.

As we saw in Gaza, Israel is also targeting water and energy supplies. In some cases, such civilian infrastructure has been directly hit, and in other cases, it’s inaccessible due to Israeli forces’ presence in the area.

In the regions of Bekaa and Baalbek, air raids have compromised essential water reservoirs and pumping stations, eliminating access to clean water to residents in the “tens of thousands,” UNICEF said. Meanwhile, damage to roads and bridges also threatens medical access to citizens in outlying regions.

6. Human Shields

Israel’s favorite go-to phrase in an attempt to divert blame for killing scores of civilians is “human shields.” In the densely populated Gaza Strip, Hamas, Israel repeatedly claims, is using civilian infrastructure and forcing residents to ignore “evacuation” orders. These claims have been repeatedly debunked, and report after report has shown it’s actually Israel who uses Palestinians as human shields.

Still, Israel, as it always does, is attempting to justify the rising death toll and the mass destruction of civilian infrastructure in Lebanon by accusing Hezbollah of using civilians as human shields.

It’s worth noting that Israel itself has often embedded in communities or set up operations near villages along its border with Lebanon, putting civilians in harm’s way.

7. White Phosphorus

Like it’s done in Gaza and previously in Lebanon, Israel is “unlawfully” using “artillery-fired white phosphorus munitions” over homes in southern Lebanon, Human Rights Watch said in early March, citing eight verified photos showing the substance being used over the town of Yohmor.

“White phosphorus is a chemical substance dispersed in artillery shells, bombs, and rockets that ignites when exposed to oxygen. It can set homes, agricultural areas, and other civilian objects on fire,” HRW wrote.

***

There are, of course, significant differences between Lebanon and Gaza: for one, Israel doesn’t control all of Lebanon’s borders, meaning it can’t fully control what goes in or out, including aid, people, and information (Beirut has been a popular base for foreign journalists for decades).

But like in Gaza, Israel has been clear about what it plans to do in Lebanon – and like with Gaza, Western nations have largely turned a blind eye as it does it.

Check out more from Zeteo:

item.title: Meet the Pro-Israel Coder Accused of Plotting to Kill a Palestinian-American Activist
item.description: On this day in 1985, the first Wrestlemania event was held at Madison Square Garden in New York. Wrestlers included Mr. T and Hulk Hogan, with Muhammad Ali refereeing and timekeeping from Liberace. Perhaps unsurprisingly, Donald Trump soon got involved, first hosting the event at one of his…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1985, the first Wrestlemania event was held at Madison Square Garden in New York. Wrestlers included Mr. T and Hulk Hogan, with Muhammad Ali refereeing and timekeeping from Liberace. Perhaps unsurprisingly, Donald Trump soon got involved, first hosting the event at one of his Atlantic City casinos in 1988.

Good morning friends! It’s Prem. Wishing a happy April Fool’s Eve to all who celebrate – and a happy Farmworkers Day to all the hardworking people who give us the gift of sustenance. May we repay the favor.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ messages leaked to Zeteo give a better picture of the man accused of seeking to kill a Palestinian-American activist; three United Nations peacekeepers were killed in Lebanon as Benjamin Netanyahu expands Israel’s illegal invasion; and the White House openly boasts about killing Iranian leaders for the crime of… disagreeing with them during nuclear negotiations.

Let’s get into it.

Caught Slacking

The man accused of plotting to assassinate Nerdeen Kiswani, a Palestinian-American activist in New York, concerned colleagues at a previous job with provocative internal communications, according to sources and messages seen by Zeteo.

Alexander Heifler, 26, was arrested last week for an alleged plot to firebomb the home of Kiswani, who lives in Brooklyn with her infant son and husband.

Heifler reportedly identified as a member of the JDL 613 Brotherhood – a New York and New Jersey-based group that has taken inspiration from the Jewish Defense League, a violent extremist organization that was listed on the FBI terrorist list.

Leaked messages obtained by Zeteo show Heifler justifying genocidal language used by Israeli government officials and feeling paranoid about his safety amid Israel’s war on Gaza.

Heifler sent the messages to colleagues at Capital One, where he worked from 2023 to 2025 as a machine learning developer, according to his LinkedIn page.

In September 2024, amid a surge of top Israeli officials invoking “Amalek” while inciting genocide in Palestine, Heifler defended the use of the term in the company Slack, in a social justice-focused sub-group. (“Amalek” refers to a Biblical story in which God commands the Israelites to exterminate an entire people.)

“Amalek is a metaphor for the enemies of the jewish people specifically the first ones to commit violence against them encouraging others to join in,” Heifler wrote. “Pointing to this archaic historical reference ignoring the contemporary metaphor doesn’t make a strong argument for genocidal intent.”

In another Slack message from March 2025, Heifler savaged the Oscar-award-winning documentary ‘No Other Land,’ an Israeli-Palestinian project that documents the Israeli destruction of Masafer Yatta, in the occupied West Bank.

Heifler called the film “Nazi propaganda.” After facing pushback, Heifler deleted “Nazi” from his message. “I revised the comment and do not hate anyone that does not hate me,” he said.

“No one hates you,” another user responded, explaining the film’s premise, saying it “shows the interest in peace, while realizing the issues faced by Palestinians in the West Bank daily.”

Heifler responded: “this was provocation. I am muting this thread now.”

In November 2023, when a pro-Israel demonstrator died after a confrontation with a pro-Palestine demonstrator in California, Heifler posted in another Slack channel dedicated to Jewish staff: “I’m starting to wonder if the US is still the right place for us. Even with the country at war I keep asking myself if I’d be safer making aliyah.” (“Aliyah” means immigrating to Israel as a Jewish person.)

In a message from February 2025, Heifler said he was “going to put together a Mordechai Eliyahu Costume this year” for Purim, a Jewish holiday.

While some dress up as Mordecai, an exile who helps save the Jewish people from annihilation, Mordechai Eliyahu was a chief Rabbi of Israel between 1983 and 1993 who in his youth helped lead Brit HaKanaim, an extremist organization that sought to make Israel run by Jewish religious law; blamed Reform Jews for the Holocaust; advocated for indiscriminately killing civilians in Gaza; and said that “the life of one yeshiva boy is worth more than the lives of 1,000 Arabs.”

The Slack messages add to the portrait of a man radicalized by a pro-Israel propaganda apparatus that has villainized Palestinians and Muslims – propelling others like Heifler to the point of enacting violence to pre-empt such fears.

The JDL, from which Heifler’s group draws inspiration, is a violent group founded in 1968 by extremist Meir Kahane. After serving a one-year “imprisonment” (in a New York hotel) for conspiring to kidnap a Soviet diplomat, bomb the Iraqi embassy in Washington, and ship arms abroad from Israel, Kahane served in the Israeli Knesset. His influence reverberates in today’s Israeli government via the likes of Itamar Ben-Gvir, the influential and violent national security minister.

On its website, JDL 613 – of which Heifler was part – boasts of collaborations with groups like Betar USA to “amplify our impact.”

Kiswani had been subject to vicious threats from pro-Israel and anti-speech groups, including Betar USA, the so-called Anti-Defamation League, Canary Mission, and the Trump-Vance administration.

The alleged plot to assassinate Kiswani came just a month after she sued Betar USA for running a coordinated campaign of harassment, violent threats, and intimidation against her.

Days before Heifler’s arrest, JDL 613’s YouTube channel posted an “emergency broadcast” calling for “no compromise” and using the language of “Amalek.”

“Don’t trust them, don’t be friends with them,” the speaker, identified as Eliezer Ben Avraham, said of Muslims, calling for “gathering intelligence… finding out where they live and work,” as well as “stalking them at their mosques,” “harassing them,” and “giving them no peace.”

Avraham framed Muslims as “Amalek,” saying “we need a couple thousand Baruch Goldsteins” – a reference to the Brooklyn-born extremist who, after moving to Israel and serving in the Israeli military, massacred 29 Palestinians in the Israeli-occupied West Bank in 1994.

Just days after Avraham’s diatribe, the Jersey-born Heifler was arrested for his alleged plot to kill Kiswani.

🇮🇷 Iran War Updates

Death tolls:

Iran: Iran’s deputy health minister told Al Jazeera that at least 1,937 people had been killed in US-Israeli attacks. Iranian officials said earlier this month that nearly 20,000 people had been injured.

Lebanon: Israel has killed at least 1,247 people, including more than 120 children, in Lebanon, according to yesterday’s figures from the country’s Health Ministry.

US troops: At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.

Israel: At least 29 people, including 10 Israeli soldiers, have been killed and thousands injured in Iranian and Hezbollah attacks.

Elsewhere in the region: Dozens of others have been killed across the region, including four Palestinian women in the occupied West Bank.

And in more Iran war news…

Tensions high: Donald Trump renewed threats to bomb Iran’s oil infrastructure – “and possibly all desalinization plants!” – seeking to pressure Tehran to “immediately” re-open the Strait of Hormuz and finalize a deal. He also continued to claim talks are ongoing. Iranian officials say they are not.

Murder boast: Press secretary Karoline Leavitt insisted Iranian leaders were “appearing more reasonable behind the scenes,” because predecessors “lied to the United States,” which was “unacceptable to the president, which is why many of the previous leaders were killed.” Observers noted that war crimes confessions are usually harder to get.

Big ask: Leavitt also said Trump was “quite interested” in having Arab countries, most under attack by Iran, help pay for a war started by the US and Israel.

Gas prices soar: Crude oil prices settled above $100 a barrel for the first time since July 2022, with average US gas prices at $4.02 – over a dollar more than before the war started. Allowing fewer than five ships a day through the Strait of Hormuz, Iran is advancing legislation to impose fees.

For Pete’s sake?: A broker for Pete Hegseth attempted to invest in US defense companies shortly before the attack on Iran, in which the defense secretary remains a driving force, the Financial Times said. The investment did not go through, and the Pentagon is angrily denying the FT story.

Deadly attacks: Explosions in southern Lebanon killed three UN peacekeepers and injured several others, shortly after Benjamin Netanyahu called on the Israeli military to expand operations in the area. The UN Security Council is set to hold an emergency meeting.

Charlie Kirk Assassination… Twist?

We don’t normally cite or quote stories from the Daily Mail here in ‘First Draft,’ but this bombshell of a piece is just too big and too juicy to ignore:

The bullet that killed conservative commentator Charlie Kirk did not match the rifle used by suspected killer Tyler Robinson, a bombshell new court filing states.

Robinson, 22, is facing capital murder charges and a potential death sentence for Kirk’s murder at Utah Valley University on September 10.

But his defense attorneys now argue that the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives ‘was unable to identify the bullet recovered at autopsy to the rifle allegedly tied to Mr Robinson.’

Sorry, what? WHAT?

🗞️ What You Need to Know

ACA threat: Republicans are considering federal healthcare cuts in a budget allocating as much as $200 billion to the Iran war and immigration enforcement, Axios reported. Analysis shows one proposal would kick 300,000 off insurance.

TSA paid: Airport misery subsided as TSA workers finally received pay delayed by the partial DHS shutdown. But union officials pointed out that not all TSA employees got overdue pay, while around 500 have quit during the shutdown.

Lit crit: Unimpressed by Pentagon attempts to get around his order to restore full press access by imposing contradictory conditions, federal judge Paul Friedman reached for literature, asking: “Is this Catch-22? Is this Kafka?”

Works for ME: The Working Families Party endorsed Graham Platner for the Democratic Senate nomination in Maine. The “combat veteran, oysterman and true fighter for working people” is well ahead of his rival, Governor Janet Mills, in the polls.

Name game: Governor Ron DeSantis, famously humiliated by Trump in the 2024 GOP primary, quietly signed a bill renaming Palm Beach International Airport in honor of his longtime tormentor.

Hat fits: Asked on NBC if she owned a MAGA hat, Usha Vance said, “I don’t really own any hats” and was “not a hat lady.” The internet was predictably quick to provide pictures of the second lady and former Democrat wearing many hats.

🧠 Pop Quiz!

South Carolina Republican Mark Sanford said yesterday he was running for his old US House seat. In 2009, as governor, he famously went missing for six days. Staff said he was “hiking the Appalachian Trail.” Where was he really?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

🌏 Anywhere But America

🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza: Israel launched airstrikes near Al-Bureij refugee camp in central Gaza yesterday, killing at least two people and injuring six others, according to the Wafa agency. Another Palestinian was reportedly killed today in strikes near Jabalia in northern Gaza.

🇮🇱 Apartheid state: Members of the Israeli parliament celebrated with champagne after approving a measure that imposes the death penalty on Palestinians convicted of deadly acts “with the intent to deny the existence of the State of Israel.”

🇦🇫🇵🇰 Tragic flooding: Around 45 people were killed and dozens injured amid devastating rainfall and flooding in Afghanistan and Pakistan, even as the two countries reignited violence following a temporary pause.

🇨🇩 Demilitarization step: Congo is moving forward with efforts to disarm the Democratic Forces for the Liberation of Rwanda, a militant group linked to Rwanda’s 1994 genocide. The move advances peace talks between the countries.

😭 WTF?!

TMZ published more pictures of GOP Senator Lindsey Graham at Disney World (!) after members of Congress left DC with the Department of Homeland Security still shut down. Graham said he went to Florida to work – TMZ showed him holding a bubble wand and getting ready to enjoy Space Mountain.

🧠 Trivia answer: In Argentina, cheating on his wife.

ICYMI From Zeteo

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly and Akshay Gokul contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘manage subscription’ in Substack, scrolling down, and toggling the button for ‘First Draft’ off.

item.title: Meet the Pro-Israel Coder Accused of Plotting to Kill a Palestinian-American Activist
item.description: On this day in 1985, the first Wrestlemania event was held at Madison Square Garden in New York. Wrestlers included Mr. T and Hulk Hogan, with Muhammad Ali refereeing and timekeeping from Liberace. Perhaps unsurprisingly, Donald Trump soon got involved, first hosting the event at one of his…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1985, the first Wrestlemania event was held at Madison Square Garden in New York. Wrestlers included Mr. T and Hulk Hogan, with Muhammad Ali refereeing and timekeeping from Liberace. Perhaps unsurprisingly, Donald Trump soon got involved, first hosting the event at one of his Atlantic City casinos in 1988.

Good morning friends! It’s Prem. Wishing a happy April Fool’s Eve to all who celebrate – and a happy Farmworkers Day to all the hardworking people who give us the gift of sustenance. May we repay the favor.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ messages leaked to Zeteo give a better picture of the man accused of seeking to kill a Palestinian-American activist; three United Nations peacekeepers were killed in Lebanon as Benjamin Netanyahu expands Israel’s illegal invasion; and the White House openly boasts about killing Iranian leaders for the crime of… disagreeing with them during nuclear negotiations.

Let’s get into it.

Caught Slacking

The man accused of plotting to assassinate Nerdeen Kiswani, a Palestinian-American activist in New York, concerned colleagues at a previous job with provocative internal communications, according to sources and messages seen by Zeteo.

Alexander Heifler, 26, was arrested last week for an alleged plot to firebomb the home of Kiswani, who lives in Brooklyn with her infant son and husband.

Heifler reportedly identified as a member of the JDL 613 Brotherhood – a New York and New Jersey-based group that has taken inspiration from the Jewish Defense League, a violent extremist organization that was listed on the FBI terrorist list.

Leaked messages obtained by Zeteo show Heifler justifying genocidal language used by Israeli government officials and feeling paranoid about his safety amid Israel’s war on Gaza.

Heifler sent the messages to colleagues at Capital One, where he worked from 2023 to 2025 as a machine learning developer, according to his LinkedIn page.

In September 2024, amid a surge of top Israeli officials invoking “Amalek” while inciting genocide in Palestine, Heifler defended the use of the term in the company Slack, in a social justice-focused sub-group. (“Amalek” refers to a Biblical story in which God commands the Israelites to exterminate an entire people.)

“Amalek is a metaphor for the enemies of the jewish people specifically the first ones to commit violence against them encouraging others to join in,” Heifler wrote. “Pointing to this archaic historical reference ignoring the contemporary metaphor doesn’t make a strong argument for genocidal intent.”

In another Slack message from March 2025, Heifler savaged the Oscar-award-winning documentary ‘No Other Land,’ an Israeli-Palestinian project that documents the Israeli destruction of Masafer Yatta, in the occupied West Bank.

Heifler called the film “Nazi propaganda.” After facing pushback, Heifler deleted “Nazi” from his message. “I revised the comment and do not hate anyone that does not hate me,” he said.

“No one hates you,” another user responded, explaining the film’s premise, saying it “shows the interest in peace, while realizing the issues faced by Palestinians in the West Bank daily.”

Heifler responded: “this was provocation. I am muting this thread now.”

In November 2023, when a pro-Israel demonstrator died after a confrontation with a pro-Palestine demonstrator in California, Heifler posted in another Slack channel dedicated to Jewish staff: “I’m starting to wonder if the US is still the right place for us. Even with the country at war I keep asking myself if I’d be safer making aliyah.” (“Aliyah” means immigrating to Israel as a Jewish person.)

In a message from February 2025, Heifler said he was “going to put together a Mordechai Eliyahu Costume this year” for Purim, a Jewish holiday.

While some dress up as Mordecai, an exile who helps save the Jewish people from annihilation, Mordechai Eliyahu was a chief Rabbi of Israel between 1983 and 1993 who in his youth helped lead Brit HaKanaim, an extremist organization that sought to make Israel run by Jewish religious law; blamed Reform Jews for the Holocaust; advocated for indiscriminately killing civilians in Gaza; and said that “the life of one yeshiva boy is worth more than the lives of 1,000 Arabs.”

The Slack messages add to the portrait of a man radicalized by a pro-Israel propaganda apparatus that has villainized Palestinians and Muslims – propelling others like Heifler to the point of enacting violence to pre-empt such fears.

The JDL, from which Heifler’s group draws inspiration, is a violent group founded in 1968 by extremist Meir Kahane. After serving a one-year “imprisonment” (in a New York hotel) for conspiring to kidnap a Soviet diplomat, bomb the Iraqi embassy in Washington, and ship arms abroad from Israel, Kahane served in the Israeli Knesset. His influence reverberates in today’s Israeli government via the likes of Itamar Ben-Gvir, the influential and violent national security minister.

On its website, JDL 613 – of which Heifler was part – boasts of collaborations with groups like Betar USA to “amplify our impact.”

Kiswani had been subject to vicious threats from pro-Israel and anti-speech groups, including Betar USA, the so-called Anti-Defamation League, Canary Mission, and the Trump-Vance administration.

The alleged plot to assassinate Kiswani came just a month after she sued Betar USA for running a coordinated campaign of harassment, violent threats, and intimidation against her.

Days before Heifler’s arrest, JDL 613’s YouTube channel posted an “emergency broadcast” calling for “no compromise” and using the language of “Amalek.”

“Don’t trust them, don’t be friends with them,” the speaker, identified as Eliezer Ben Avraham, said of Muslims, calling for “gathering intelligence… finding out where they live and work,” as well as “stalking them at their mosques,” “harassing them,” and “giving them no peace.”

Avraham framed Muslims as “Amalek,” saying “we need a couple thousand Baruch Goldsteins” – a reference to the Brooklyn-born extremist who, after moving to Israel and serving in the Israeli military, massacred 29 Palestinians in the Israeli-occupied West Bank in 1994.

Just days after Avraham’s diatribe, the Jersey-born Heifler was arrested for his alleged plot to kill Kiswani.

🇮🇷 Iran War Updates

Death tolls:

Iran: Iran’s deputy health minister told Al Jazeera that at least 1,937 people had been killed in US-Israeli attacks. Iranian officials said earlier this month that nearly 20,000 people had been injured.

Lebanon: Israel has killed at least 1,247 people, including more than 120 children, in Lebanon, according to yesterday’s figures from the country’s Health Ministry.

US troops: At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.

Israel: At least 29 people, including 10 Israeli soldiers, have been killed and thousands injured in Iranian and Hezbollah attacks.

Elsewhere in the region: Dozens of others have been killed across the region, including four Palestinian women in the occupied West Bank.

And in more Iran war news…

Tensions high: Donald Trump renewed threats to bomb Iran’s oil infrastructure – “and possibly all desalinization plants!” – seeking to pressure Tehran to “immediately” re-open the Strait of Hormuz and finalize a deal. He also continued to claim talks are ongoing. Iranian officials say they are not.

Murder boast: Press secretary Karoline Leavitt insisted Iranian leaders were “appearing more reasonable behind the scenes,” because predecessors “lied to the United States,” which was “unacceptable to the president, which is why many of the previous leaders were killed.” Observers noted that war crimes confessions are usually harder to get.

Big ask: Leavitt also said Trump was “quite interested” in having Arab countries, most under attack by Iran, help pay for a war started by the US and Israel.

Gas prices soar: Crude oil prices settled above $100 a barrel for the first time since July 2022, with average US gas prices at $4.02 – over a dollar more than before the war started. Allowing fewer than five ships a day through the Strait of Hormuz, Iran is advancing legislation to impose fees.

Deadly attacks: Explosions in southern Lebanon killed three UN peacekeepers and injured several others, shortly after Benjamin Netanyahu called on the Israeli military to expand operations in the area. The UN Security Council is set to hold an emergency meeting.

We don’t normally cite or quote stories from the Daily Mail here in ‘First Draft,’ but this bombshell of a piece is just too big and too juicy to ignore:

The bullet that killed conservative commentator Charlie Kirk did not match the rifle used by suspected killer Tyler Robinson, a bombshell new court filing states.

Sorry, what? WHAT?

🗞️ What You Need to Know

ACA threat: Republicans are considering federal healthcare cuts in a budget allocating as much as $200 billion to the Iran war and immigration enforcement, Axios reported. Analysis shows one proposal would kick 300,000 off insurance.

TSA paid: Airport misery subsided as TSA workers finally received pay delayed by the partial DHS shutdown. But union officials pointed out that not all TSA employees got overdue pay, while around 500 have quit during the shutdown.

🧠 Pop Quiz!

South Carolina Republican Mark Sanford said yesterday he was running for his old US House seat. In 2009, as governor, he famously went missing for six days. Staff said he was “hiking the Appalachian Trail.” Where was he really?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

🌏 Anywhere But America

🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza: Israel launched airstrikes near Al-Bureij refugee camp in central Gaza yesterday, killing at least two people and injuring six others, according to the Wafa agency. Another Palestinian was reportedly killed today in strikes near Jabalia in northern Gaza.

TMZ published more pictures of GOP Senator Lindsey Graham at Disney World (!) after members of Congress left DC with the Department of Homeland Security still shut down. Graham said he went to Florida to work – TMZ showed him holding a bubble wand and getting ready to enjoy Space Mountain.

🧠 Trivia answer: In Argentina, cheating on his wife.
